walkthrough.title: Choose Sides and Powers
walkthrough.text: Axis & Allies 1942 is played by two to five players divided into two sides. The Axis consists of Germany and Japan. The Allies consist of the Soviet Union, the United Kingdom, and the United States. In a five-player game, each player controls one power. With fewer players, some will control multiple powers, but they must all be on the same side. Decide who plays which side, then assign specific powers. Keep each power's income and units separate even if one player controls multiple powers.

walkthrough.title: Unfold the Game Board
walkthrough.text: Lay out the large game board in the center of the table. This is a map of the world circa 1942, divided into territories on land and numbered sea zones at sea. Each territory shows a border color indicating which power controls it at game start, along with that power's emblem. Most territories have an IPC income value ranging from 1 to 12. Note that the board wraps around horizontally -- territories and sea zones on the right edge are adjacent to those on the left edge. Neutral territories like Turkey, Mongolia, and Sahara are impassable.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Setup Cards
walkthrough.text: Each player takes the setup card for their power. The setup card shows your power's name, unit color, and emblem, along with a complete list of where every starting unit goes on the board. The five powers and their colors are: Soviet Union (maroon), Germany (gray), United Kingdom (tan), Japan (orange), and United States (green). Read through your setup card carefully before placing units.

walkthrough.title: Place Starting Units on the Board
walkthrough.text: Using your setup card as a guide, place all of your power's plastic units on the board in their listed territories and sea zones. This is the most time-consuming part of setup, so be methodical. Each power has unique sculpts representing historical units -- for example, the US has Sherman tanks, P-38 fighters, and B-17 bombers, while Germany has Panther tanks, BF-109 fighters, and JU-88 bombers. Use plastic chips to save space in crowded territories: gray chips represent 1 unit, green chips represent 3, and red chips represent 5. Stack them with a unit on top.

walkthrough.title: Place National Control Markers
walkthrough.text: Each player takes all the national control markers featuring their power's emblem. These round tokens mark conquered territories during the game. At setup, you do not need to place them on territories you already control -- those are indicated by the printed border colors. You will place control markers later when you capture enemy territories. Keep your markers in a pile near your play area.

walkthrough.title: Set National Production Levels
walkthrough.text: Look at the national production chart on the game board. Each power places one control marker on the number matching their starting income. The starting income levels are: Soviet Union 24 IPCs, Germany 41 IPCs, United Kingdom 31 IPCs, Japan 30 IPCs, and United States 42 IPCs. This chart tracks each power's income throughout the game as territories change hands.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Starting IPCs
walkthrough.text: Industrial Production Credits (IPCs) are the currency of the game. Each power begins with IPCs in their treasury equal to their starting national production level. So Germany starts with 41 IPCs, the United States with 42, and so on. Designate one player as the banker to track each power's IPC treasury on paper or by another agreed method. Powers will spend IPCs to purchase units and collect IPCs as income from controlled territories.

walkthrough.title: Identify Victory Cities
walkthrough.text: There are thirteen victory cities on the map that are crucial to winning. The Allies start controlling seven: Washington, London, Leningrad, Moscow, Calcutta, Honolulu, and San Francisco. The Axis start controlling six: Berlin, Paris, Rome, Shanghai, Manila, and Tokyo. Before you begin, agree on the victory condition. Standard Victory requires the Axis to hold 9 cities or the Allies to hold 10 at the end of a complete round. Total Victory requires one side to hold all 13. If no agreement is made, Standard Victory applies.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Battle Strip and Dice
walkthrough.text: Place the battle strip card in a central location where all players can reach it. The battle strip has two sides labeled Attacker and Defender, with columns listing unit types and their combat values. When combat occurs, players line up their units on the appropriate columns. Also set out the casualty strip, which is used to mark units that have been hit but still get to return fire. Have plenty of dice ready -- you will roll a lot of them.

walkthrough.title: Note the Canals and Special Rules
walkthrough.text: Before play begins, make sure everyone knows about the two canals. The Panama Canal connects sea zone 19 (Pacific) to sea zone 18 (Atlantic) -- whoever controls Central America controls this canal. The Suez Canal connects sea zone 17 (Mediterranean) to sea zone 34 (Indian Ocean) -- your side must control both Egypt and Trans-Jordan. Sea units can only use a canal if your side controlled it at the start of your turn. Air units are unaffected by canal control. Also note the optional Turkey rule: sea zone 16 (Black Sea straits) may be closed to naval traffic.

walkthrough.title: Understand the Turn Order
walkthrough.text: Axis & Allies 1942 is played in rounds. Each round, the five powers take turns in a fixed order: 1. Soviet Union, 2. Germany, 3. United Kingdom, 4. Japan, 5. United States. After the US turn, check for victory. Each power's turn has six phases in order: Purchase Units, Combat Move, Conduct Combat, Noncombat Move, Mobilize New Units, and Collect Income. You must collect income if able, but all other phases are voluntary. Once you understand this sequence, you are ready to play.

walkthrough.title: Final Check and Begin
walkthrough.text: Do a final check: every player should have their units on the board matching their setup card, a control marker on the national production chart, and IPCs in their treasury equal to their starting income. Make sure the victory condition is agreed upon and everyone understands the turn order. The Soviet Union goes first. Take a moment to survey the board -- the Axis are at the peak of their power in 1942, controlling vast swaths of Europe and the Pacific. The Allies must coordinate across three powers to push back. Good luck, commanders.

summary.title: Choose Sides and Powers
bullets: Axis: Germany + Japan | Allies: Soviet Union + United Kingdom + United States
2-5 players; if fewer than 5, one player controls multiple powers on the same side
Keep each power's income and units tracked separately

summary.title: Unfold the Game Board
bullets: World map divided into territories (land) and numbered sea zones
Territory borders are color-coded by controlling power
Board wraps horizontally; neutral territories are impassable

summary.title: Distribute Setup Cards
bullets: Each power has a setup card listing all starting unit positions
Colors: Soviet Union (maroon), Germany (gray), UK (tan), Japan (orange), US (green)

summary.title: Place Starting Units on the Board
bullets: Place all plastic units per setup card in listed territories and sea zones
Use plastic chips for crowded spaces: gray=1, green=3, red=5
Each power has unique historical sculpts (Sherman, Panther, Spitfire, Zero, etc.)

summary.title: Place National Control Markers
bullets: Take all control markers with your power's emblem
Used to mark territories you capture during the game
Starting territories are shown by printed border colors

summary.title: Set National Production Levels
bullets: Place control marker on national production chart at starting income
USSR: 24, Germany: 41, UK: 31, Japan: 30, USA: 42

summary.title: Distribute Starting IPCs
bullets: Each power's treasury starts equal to its national production level
Designate a banker to track IPC treasuries
IPCs are spent to buy units and collected as income each turn

summary.title: Identify Victory Cities
bullets: 13 victory cities total: Allies start with 7, Axis start with 6
Standard Victory: Axis need 9, Allies need 10 (checked after US turn)
Total Victory (optional): one side controls all 13

summary.title: Set Up the Battle Strip and Dice
bullets: Battle strip has Attacker/Defender sides with unit combat values
Casualty strip marks units hit but still able to return fire
Have plenty of six-sided dice available

summary.title: Note the Canals and Special Rules
bullets: Panama Canal: sea zones 19-18, controlled by whoever holds Central America
Suez Canal: sea zones 17-34, requires control of both Egypt and Trans-Jordan
Must control canal at START of your turn to use it; air units unaffected

summary.title: Understand the Turn Order
bullets: Turn order: 1. USSR, 2. Germany, 3. UK, 4. Japan, 5. USA, then check victory
Each turn: Purchase > Combat Move > Conduct Combat > Noncombat Move > Mobilize > Collect Income
Collect Income is mandatory; all other phases are voluntary

summary.title: Final Check and Begin
bullets: Verify all units placed per setup cards, production markers set, IPCs distributed
Confirm victory condition (Standard or Total)
Soviet Union takes the first turn

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Purchase Units
walkthrough.text: The first phase of your turn is purchasing new units. Look at the mobilization zone chart on the game board -- it lists every unit you can buy with its IPC cost. You can purchase as many units as you can afford and will be able to mobilize later. You do not have to spend all your IPCs. Place purchased units in the mobilization zone on the board -- you cannot use them yet. You can also pay 1 IPC per point to repair damage on your industrial complexes. Key unit costs: Infantry 3, Artillery 4, Tank 6, AA Artillery 5, Fighter 10, Bomber 12, Submarine 6, Transport 7, Destroyer 8, Cruiser 12, Aircraft Carrier 14, Battleship 20, Industrial Complex 15.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Combat Move
walkthrough.text: In the Combat Move phase, you move units into hostile spaces to attack. All combat movement happens simultaneously -- you cannot move a unit, fight, then move it again. Land and sea units can move up to their movement value. Most units must stop when entering a hostile space. Land units stop immediately upon entering enemy territory. Sea units stop when entering a sea zone with enemy surface warships (battleships, carriers, cruisers, or destroyers). However, enemy submarines and transports alone do not make a sea zone hostile. You can move through friendly spaces en route to hostile ones.

walkthrough.title: Air Unit Movement
walkthrough.text: Air units have special movement rules. A fighter has move 4 and a bomber has move 6, but they must reserve enough movement to return to a safe landing spot during the Noncombat Move phase. So a fighter attacking 2 spaces away uses 2 movement going in and has 2 remaining to fly home. Air units can fly through hostile territories and sea zones freely -- they only engage in combat at their destination. Fighters can land on friendly aircraft carriers. Bombers must land in friendly territories. You cannot deliberately send air units beyond their range of a valid landing spot.

walkthrough.title: Special Combat Movement
walkthrough.text: Several units have special movement abilities. Tanks can blitz: a tank moving through an unoccupied hostile territory as its first space of movement claims that territory and can continue to a second space. If the first territory has any enemy units (even AA guns or an industrial complex), the tank must stop. Submarines can move through hostile sea zones without stopping, unless an enemy destroyer is present. Aircraft carriers launch their fighters before moving -- fighters move independently. Transports can load units from friendly coastal territories along their route.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: Conduct Combat -- Strategic Bombing Raids
walkthrough.text: Combat is resolved in three steps: strategic bombing raids first, then amphibious assaults, then general combat. In a strategic bombing raid, your bombers directly attack an enemy industrial complex. The complex has built-in air defense: it rolls one die per attacking bomber, and each roll of 1 destroys a bomber. Surviving bombers each roll one die, and that many damage points are placed under the complex as chips. Total damage cannot exceed twice the territory's IPC value. Each damage chip reduces the complex's production capacity by one unit. The optional rule allows escort fighters and interceptor fighters to battle before the bombing.

walkthrough.title: Amphibious Assaults
walkthrough.text: An amphibious assault is an attack on a coastal territory from the sea. You must announce these during Combat Move. The sequence is: first, sea combat if enemy surface warships are in the sea zone. Second, if there was no sea combat, battleships and cruisers can bombard -- each rolls one die using its attack value (battleships hit on 4 or less, cruisers on 3 or less). The number of bombarding ships cannot exceed the number of land units being offloaded. Third, land combat occurs using general combat rules. Seaborne units that offloaded cannot retreat. Overland attackers can retreat together to a territory from which at least one entered.

walkthrough.title: General Combat -- Setup and Submarine Phase
walkthrough.text: General combat resolves all remaining battles. Place attacking and defending units on their respective sides of the battle strip, aligned with their combat values. Combat proceeds in steps. Step 1: place units. Step 2 (sea battles only): submarines choose to either make a Surprise Strike or submerge. If no enemy destroyer is present, attacking subs roll first -- each hit on 2 or less, and the defender assigns hits to sea units. Then defending subs roll -- each hit on 1. Casualties from surprise strikes are removed immediately. If a sub submerges, it returns to the board and exits combat entirely. An enemy destroyer cancels Surprise Strike, Submersible, and the immunity to air units.

walkthrough.title: General Combat -- Attack and Defend Rolls
walkthrough.text: Step 3: all attacking units fire. Roll one die per unit. A unit scores a hit if you roll equal to or less than its attack value. Infantry attack at 1 (or 2 if paired with artillery), artillery at 2, tanks at 3, fighters at 3, bombers at 4, submarines at 2, destroyers at 2, cruisers at 3, carriers at 1, battleships at 4. The defender chooses which units take hits and places them behind the casualty strip. Step 4: all defending units fire, including those behind the casualty strip. Defense values: infantry 2, artillery 2, tanks 3, fighters 4, bombers 1, submarines 1, destroyers 2, cruisers 3, carriers 2, battleships 4. Attacker removes their casualties.

walkthrough.title: General Combat -- Resolution
walkthrough.text: Step 5: remove the defender's casualties from the casualty strip. Step 6: decide whether to press the attack or retreat. Combat rounds repeat (steps 2-5) until one side loses all units or the attacker retreats. The attacker can retreat all land and sea units to a single adjacent friendly space from which at least one attacking unit came. Air units remain in the contested space temporarily and complete their retreat during Noncombat Move. The defender never retreats. If the attacker wins in a territory and has surviving land units, they capture it -- place a control marker and adjust production levels.

walkthrough.title: Antiaircraft Artillery in Combat
walkthrough.text: Antiaircraft artillery (AA guns) fire at attacking air units before the first round of combat. Each AA gun can fire up to 3 shots, but each attacking air unit can only be targeted once. So the total AA dice rolled equals the lesser of (3 times the number of AA guns) or (the number of attacking air units). Each roll of 1 destroys one air unit immediately -- those planes never get to fight. AA guns have no regular combat value (they roll 0 in normal combat steps) but can be taken as casualties. If a territory has only AA guns and no combat units, attacking ground forces auto-destroy them, but air units must survive the AA fire first. AA guns can only move during Noncombat Move.

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Noncombat Move
walkthrough.text: After combat, you can move any units that did not participate in combat this turn. You also land all surviving aircraft from combat. Land units can move into friendly territories only -- they cannot enter or pass through hostile territory. Air units use their remaining movement (total move value minus spaces already used in Combat Move) to reach a safe landing spot. Fighters land in friendly territories or on carriers. Bombers land in friendly territories only. Air units cannot land in territories captured this turn. Submarines can move through hostile sea zones in this phase but must stop if they enter a sea zone with an enemy destroyer. This is also the only phase AA guns can move.

walkthrough.title: Phase 5: Mobilize New Units
walkthrough.text: Place all units you purchased earlier from the mobilization zone onto the board. Land units and bombers go into territories with your industrial complexes. Sea units go into sea zones adjacent to territories with your industrial complexes. Fighters go into territories with your industrial complexes or onto your carriers in adjacent sea zones. Each industrial complex can produce units up to the IPC value of its territory, minus any damage chips. You can only use complexes you controlled at the start of your turn -- not ones you just captured or purchased this turn. New sea units can enter hostile sea zones without triggering combat.

walkthrough.title: Phase 6: Collect Income
walkthrough.text: The final phase of your turn: collect IPCs equal to your national production level, as shown on the production chart. This is the total IPC value of all territories you control. There is one critical exception: if your capital is under enemy control, you cannot collect income. Capitals are Washington, Moscow, London, Berlin, and Tokyo. When a capital is captured, the conqueror seizes all unspent IPCs from that power's treasury. The conquered power stays in the game but skips Purchase, Mobilize, and Collect Income phases until their capital is liberated. Powers cannot lend or give IPCs to allies.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: After the United States completes its turn, check the victory condition. Under Standard Victory, the Axis win if they control 9 or more victory cities, and the Allies win if they control 10 or more. Under Total Victory, one side must control all 13. Victory is checked only at the end of a complete round, after the US turn. The game continues round after round until one side achieves the agreed condition. A typical game can last 3 to 6 hours. Remember: controlling territories funds your war machine, but only victory cities determine the winner.

walkthrough.title: Key Rules to Remember
walkthrough.text: A few rules that often trip up new players. First, liberating a territory: if you capture a territory originally controlled by an ally, the original controller gets it back (unless their capital is enemy-held). Second, multinational forces can defend together but cannot attack together -- each power fights only on its own turn. Third, transports have zero combat value and are chosen as casualties last. Fourth, battleships take two hits to destroy -- flip them on their side after the first hit, and they repair at the end of combat if they survive. Fifth, industrial complexes can never be destroyed, only damaged. Sixth, fighters on allied carriers are cargo and cannot participate in combat.

summary.title: Phase 1: Purchase Units
bullets: Spend IPCs to buy units from the mobilization zone chart
Key costs: Infantry 3, Artillery 4, Tank 6, Fighter 10, Bomber 12, Sub 6, Transport 7, Destroyer 8, Cruiser 12, Carrier 14, Battleship 20
Pay 1 IPC per chip to repair industrial complex damage
Purchased units placed in mobilization zone -- deployed later in Phase 5

summary.title: Phase 2: Combat Move
bullets: Move units into hostile spaces (enemy-controlled or containing enemy surface warships)
All combat movement is simultaneous -- no move-fight-move
Land units stop upon entering hostile territory
Enemy subs and transports alone do NOT make a sea zone hostile

summary.title: Air Unit Movement
bullets: Fighters: move 4, attack 3, defense 4 | Bombers: move 6, attack 4, defense 1
Must reserve movement to return to a landing spot in Noncombat Move
Can fly through hostile spaces without stopping
Fighters can land on carriers; bombers must land in friendly territories

summary.title: Special Combat Movement
bullets: Tanks blitz: move through unoccupied hostile territory, claim it, continue to 2nd space
Subs pass through hostile sea zones unless an enemy destroyer is present
Carriers launch fighters before moving; fighters move independently
Transports load cargo from friendly coastal territories along their route

summary.title: Phase 3: Strategic Bombing Raids
bullets: Bombers attack enemy industrial complexes directly
Complex air defense: 1 die per bomber, hit on 1 = bomber destroyed
Surviving bombers each roll 1 die = damage chips placed under complex
Max damage = 2x territory IPC value; each chip reduces production by 1

summary.title: Amphibious Assaults
bullets: Sequence: sea combat (if enemy warships) > bombardment > land combat
Bombardment: battleships hit on 4-, cruisers hit on 3-; max ships = offloaded units
No bombardment if any sea combat occurred (even attacking subs/transports)
Seaborne units cannot retreat; overland attackers can retreat together

summary.title: General Combat -- Submarine Phase
bullets: Subs choose: Surprise Strike or Submerge (before other units fire)
Surprise Strike: attacking subs hit on 2-, defending subs hit on 1
Submerged subs exit combat immediately, return to the board
Enemy destroyer cancels Surprise Strike, Submersible, and air immunity

summary.title: General Combat -- Attack and Defend Rolls
bullets: Attack values: Inf 1 (2 with artillery), Art 2, Tank 3, Ftr 3, Bmr 4, Sub 2, DD 2, CA 3, CV 1, BB 4
Defense values: Inf 2, Art 2, Tank 3, Ftr 4, Bmr 1, Sub 1, DD 2, CA 3, CV 2, BB 4
Roll 1 die per unit; hit if roll <= combat value
Defender's casualties still return fire before being removed

summary.title: General Combat -- Resolution
bullets: Repeat combat rounds until one side eliminated or attacker retreats
Attacker retreats all units to a single adjacent friendly space
Defender never retreats
Capture territory with surviving land units; place control marker, adjust production

summary.title: Antiaircraft Artillery in Combat
bullets: AA guns fire before first combat round at attacking air units
Dice = lesser of (3 x AA guns) or (# attacking air units); hit on 1
Destroyed planes removed immediately, never get to fight
AA guns have 0 combat value; can only move in Noncombat Move phase

summary.title: Phase 4: Noncombat Move
bullets: Move units that did not participate in combat; land all surviving aircraft
Land units: friendly territories only, cannot enter hostile territory
Air units: use remaining movement to reach safe landing spot (not captured territory)
Only phase where AA guns can move; subs can pass through hostile sea zones

summary.title: Phase 5: Mobilize New Units
bullets: Deploy purchased units at industrial complexes you controlled at start of turn
Production limit per complex = territory IPC value minus damage chips
Sea units placed in adjacent sea zones; fighters can go on carriers in adjacent zones
Cannot use just-captured or just-purchased complexes this turn

summary.title: Phase 6: Collect Income
bullets: Collect IPCs equal to national production level
Cannot collect if your capital is enemy-held
Capturing a capital seizes all unspent IPCs from that power
Powers cannot lend or give IPCs to allies

summary.title: Winning the Game
bullets: Checked after US turn: Standard Victory = Axis 9 / Allies 10 victory cities
Total Victory (optional) = one side holds all 13
Only victory cities matter for winning, not territory count or income

summary.title: Key Rules to Remember
bullets: Liberated territories revert to original controller (unless their capital is enemy-held)
Multinational forces defend together but cannot attack together
Transports: 0 combat value, chosen as casualty last
Battleships take 2 hits to destroy; industrial complexes can never be destroyed

walkthrough.title: Welcome to the Eastern Front
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted first turn for the Soviet Union -- the first power to act in every round of Axis & Allies 1942. The USSR is in a precarious position: Germany has massive forces on its western border, and Japan threatens from the east. This tutorial will teach you the six-phase turn sequence, demonstrate combat, and highlight common beginner mistakes. Follow along even if you are playing a different power -- the turn structure is identical for everyone.

walkthrough.title: Soviet Starting Position
walkthrough.text: Take a moment to survey the Soviet position. The USSR starts with 24 IPCs in income and 24 IPCs in the treasury. Moscow is your capital and your primary industrial complex with an IPC value of 8, meaning it can produce up to 8 units per turn. You also have an industrial complex in Karelia (worth 2 IPCs). Soviet forces are spread across the western front facing Germany and the eastern territories facing Japan. Key defensive positions include Karelia, Caucasus, and the territories around Moscow.

walkthrough.title: Phase 1: Purchase Units
walkthrough.text: With 24 IPCs to spend, you need to think about what the USSR needs most. Germany will attack you hard on their turn, so you need defenders. A strong opening purchase is 8 infantry (8 x 3 = 24 IPCs). Infantry are the most cost-efficient defenders in the game: each one defends at 2 and costs only 3 IPCs. That is a defense value of 2 for 3 IPCs -- no other unit matches that ratio. Place your 8 purchased infantry in the mobilization zone. You will deploy them at the end of your turn.

walkthrough.title: Why Infantry Are King for Defense
walkthrough.text: Let us compare. A tank costs 6 IPCs and defends at 3. That is 2 IPCs per point of defense. An infantry costs 3 IPCs and defends at 2. That is 1.5 IPCs per point of defense. So two infantry (6 IPCs total) give you defense value 4 on two separate rolls, while one tank (6 IPCs) gives you only defense value 3 on one roll. Two infantry absorb two hits; one tank absorbs only one. When you are the defender under pressure, quantity of cheap infantry beats quality of expensive units almost every time.

walkthrough.title: Phase 2: Combat Move -- Planning Attacks
walkthrough.text: Now for Combat Move. The USSR can attack on several fronts, but you need to be strategic. You do not want to throw away units in hopeless attacks because Germany hits back next turn. Look for battles where you have overwhelming force or where the strategic value justifies the risk. A common Soviet opening is to attack Ukraine and possibly West Russia if Germany left them lightly defended. The goal is to trade territories for income and weaken German forward positions while keeping your main forces intact.

walkthrough.title: Executing an Attack on Ukraine
walkthrough.text: Let us say you decide to attack Ukraine. Move infantry, artillery, and tanks from Caucasus and surrounding territories into Ukraine. Also send fighters from Moscow to support the attack -- but remember, your fighters have move 4, and they need to fly back to a friendly territory afterward. Moscow is within range for the return trip, so your fighters are safe. Move all your attacking units into Ukraine. This is your combat move for that battle.

walkthrough.title: Phase 3: Conduct Combat -- The Ukraine Battle
walkthrough.text: Time to fight. Place your attacking units on the Attacker side of the battle strip. The German defender places their units on the Defender side. Let us say you are attacking with 3 infantry, 1 artillery, 2 tanks, and 1 fighter against 2 German infantry, 1 tank, and 1 fighter. Your attack rolls: 3 infantry at 1 (but one is supported by artillery, so it attacks at 2), 1 artillery at 2, 2 tanks at 3, 1 fighter at 3. Roll dice for each group and count hits. The defender then rolls: 2 infantry at 2, 1 tank at 3, 1 fighter at 4.

walkthrough.title: Resolving Casualties
walkthrough.text: Let us say the attacker scored 3 hits and the defender scored 2 hits. The defender chooses 3 of their units to take as casualties -- they place them behind the casualty strip. Those units still got to fire in step 4 (defending units fire) before being removed in step 5. The attacker removes their 2 casualties immediately. As the attacker losing units, you want to lose your cheapest units first: infantry before artillery, artillery before tanks, tanks before fighters. This preserves your expensive firepower for subsequent rounds.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Battle
walkthrough.text: If you destroyed all defending units while still having attackers remaining, you win the battle. Congratulations -- you have captured Ukraine. Place a Soviet control marker on the territory and adjust the national production chart: USSR income goes up by Ukraine's IPC value, and Germany's income goes down by the same amount. Leave your surviving units in Ukraine. If you had not destroyed all defenders, you would face a choice in step 6: press the attack (roll again) or retreat all units to a single friendly territory from which at least one entered.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake 1: Forgetting AA Gun Fire
walkthrough.text: Here is a mistake new players make constantly. Say you are attacking a territory that has an antiaircraft artillery unit and you sent fighters or bombers. BEFORE the first round of combat, the AA gun fires at your air units. Each AA gun fires up to 3 shots, hitting on a roll of 1. If the territory has 1 AA gun and you sent 2 fighters, 2 dice are rolled (one per air unit). Each 1 rolled destroys a fighter immediately -- it never gets to attack. Many new players forget this step entirely and just roll combat normally. Always check for AA guns before rolling your first attack.

walkthrough.title: Phase 4: Noncombat Move
walkthrough.text: After combat, you can move any units that did not fight. This is critical for the USSR -- you need to reinforce your front lines. Move infantry and other units from safe rear territories toward the front. Pull your fighters back from combat zones to territories where they are safe but can still reach future battles. Move AA guns forward to protect key territories (remember, AA guns can only move during this phase). A good Soviet habit is to stack units in Moscow, Caucasus, and Karelia as a defensive wall.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake 2: Transport Loading Rules
walkthrough.text: Even though the USSR does not use transports much on turn 1, this is the most commonly misplayed rule in the game. A transport can carry one land unit plus one additional infantry (so 2 infantry, or 1 tank/artillery/AA gun + 1 infantry). Loading and offloading counts as the land unit's entire movement -- it cannot walk and then load. During Combat Move, loaded transports MUST offload into a hostile territory as an amphibious assault. You cannot load during Combat Move and then just sit at sea. And critically, if the sea zone has enemy submarines and you have no warship escort, you cannot offload.

walkthrough.title: Phase 5: Mobilize New Units
walkthrough.text: Now deploy the 8 infantry you purchased at the start of your turn. You can place them at any industrial complex you controlled at the start of your turn. Moscow (IPC value 8) can receive up to 8 units. Karelia (IPC value 2) can receive up to 2. For your first turn, placing most infantry in Moscow gives you a strong central defense. From Moscow, they can reinforce any threatened front next turn. You could split them, placing 2 in Karelia and 6 in Moscow, to immediately strengthen the northern front.

walkthrough.title: Phase 6: Collect Income
walkthrough.text: Count up the IPC values of all territories you now control, including any you captured this turn (like Ukraine). Your control marker on the national production chart should already reflect these changes from combat. Collect that many IPCs and add them to your treasury. If you started with 24 income and captured Ukraine (worth 3 IPCs), your new income is 27. But wait -- if Germany captures some of your territory on their turn, your income will drop. This is the economic tug-of-war at the heart of Axis & Allies.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistake 3: Amphibious Assault Bombardment Errors
walkthrough.text: This rule catches veteran players too. When conducting an amphibious assault, your battleships and cruisers can bombard the coastal territory -- but ONLY if there was NO sea combat in that sea zone. If you had to fight even a single enemy submarine or transport in the sea zone (and chose to attack them rather than ignore them), your battleships and cruisers lose their bombardment. Also, the number of ships that can bombard is limited to the number of land units being offloaded. Three infantry offloading means a maximum of three ships can bombard. Plan your naval positioning carefully.

walkthrough.title: Germany's Turn -- The Hammer Falls
walkthrough.text: Your turn is over. Now Germany goes with 41 IPCs and the most powerful military on the board. Watch carefully as Germany executes the same six phases. They will likely attack your positions in Ukraine, Karelia, and possibly the Eastern Front. This is where you see if your defensive setup was smart. Notice how Germany uses combined arms -- infantry with artillery support (boosting infantry attack from 1 to 2), tanks for blitzing through empty territories, and aircraft for devastating firepower. Your wall of infantry should slow them down.

walkthrough.title: United Kingdom's Turn -- Opening the Second Front
walkthrough.text: After Germany, the United Kingdom takes its turn. The UK has 31 IPCs but its forces are scattered: defending Britain, holding Egypt and India, and maintaining a navy. A common UK strategy is to build transports and infantry to threaten Western Europe with amphibious assaults, forcing Germany to divert defenders from the Eastern Front. Watch how the UK uses the six phases with a naval focus -- purchasing ships, moving the fleet, and potentially launching an amphibious assault against a weakly defended Axis territory.

walkthrough.title: Japan's Turn -- The Pacific War
walkthrough.text: Japan goes fourth with 30 IPCs. Japan's situation mirrors Germany's -- they are at their peak, with a massive navy and expanding empire. Japan will likely push into China, threaten India, and deal with the US Pacific fleet. Pay attention to how Japan uses transports to move infantry across the Pacific and how carrier-based fighters project power across vast sea zones. Japan's strategic challenge is deciding how much to pressure the USSR from the east versus expanding in the Pacific.

walkthrough.title: United States' Turn -- The Arsenal of Democracy
walkthrough.text: The US goes last with 42 IPCs -- the highest starting income. But the US starts slow: most forces are in the western hemisphere, far from the fighting. The US must decide early: go Atlantic first (help UK pressure Germany) or Pacific first (contain Japan). A common opening is to build a mix of naval and ground forces. The US can afford aircraft carriers, fighters, and transports to project power across either ocean. After the US turn, check the victory city count. If neither side has won, begin a new round with the Soviet Union again.

walkthrough.title: Understanding the Economic Cycle
walkthrough.text: After one full round, you should see the economic engine of the game. Territories produce IPCs. IPCs buy units. Units capture territories. More territories produce more IPCs. This cycle is the heartbeat of Axis & Allies. The Axis start with a military advantage but the Allies start with a combined income advantage (24 + 31 + 42 = 97 Allied vs 41 + 30 = 71 Axis). If the Allies can survive the early onslaught and maintain their economic edge, they will eventually outproduce the Axis. Germany and Japan must strike fast before the Allied war machine reaches full production.

walkthrough.title: The Infantry Push Mechanic
walkthrough.text: Here is a fundamental tactic you will use constantly: the infantry push. Each turn, you buy infantry and push them toward the front lines. Behind the front, you keep expensive units like tanks and fighters ready to reinforce wherever the enemy attacks. The infantry absorb hits (because they are cheap and defend at 2), while the tanks and fighters deliver the punishing counterattacks. This is how the Soviets survive against Germany and how the Allies slowly grind the Axis down -- wave after wave of infantry backed by mobile striking power.

walkthrough.title: Turn 2: USSR Again
walkthrough.text: Round 2 begins and the USSR takes another turn. By now, Germany has likely recaptured some territory. That is expected -- the Eastern Front is a grinding back-and-forth battle. On your second purchase, consider adding 1 artillery to your infantry buys. Artillery costs 4 IPCs but boosts one infantry's attack from 1 to 2, making your counterattacks more effective. A typical USSR turn 2 buy might be 1 artillery and 6 infantry (4 + 18 = 22 IPCs, saving 2 from leftover). Start applying the full six-phase sequence with confidence now.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: You have now walked through a complete round plus the start of a second round. You understand the six-phase turn sequence, combat resolution with the battle strip, special rules for AA guns, transports, and bombardment, and the economic engine that drives the war. The three common mistakes to watch for: forgetting AA gun fire before combat, mishandling transport loading rules, and losing bombardment rights by engaging in sea combat. These cover the most frequently misplayed situations in the game.

walkthrough.title: Before You Go to War
walkthrough.text:
Before diving into your next full game, here are three things that will help.

First -- check out General Tips for strategic fundamentals and common mistakes.

Second -- reference the Appendix for a complete guide to every unit type, combat values, and special abilities.

And most importantly -- ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Whether it is a transport rule, a combat question, or strategy advice, your GM Guide has you covered. Have fun, commanders.

summary.title: Welcome to the Eastern Front
bullets: Scripted tutorial following the Soviet Union's first turn
Covers all six phases of a turn with practical examples
Highlights 3 common beginner mistakes

summary.title: Soviet Starting Position
bullets: USSR starts with 24 IPCs income and 24 IPCs in treasury
Moscow (IPC 8) is the primary industrial complex
Forces spread across western front (vs Germany) and east (vs Japan)

summary.title: Phase 1: Purchase Units
bullets: Buy 8 infantry for 24 IPCs (3 each) -- maximum defensive value
Infantry defend at 2 for just 3 IPCs -- best cost-efficiency in the game
Place purchased units in the mobilization zone

summary.title: Why Infantry Are King for Defense
bullets: Infantry: 1.5 IPCs per defense point | Tank: 2.0 IPCs per defense point
Two infantry (6 IPCs) = defense 4, absorb 2 hits
One tank (6 IPCs) = defense 3, absorbs 1 hit
Quantity of cheap defenders beats expensive units under pressure

summary.title: Phase 2: Combat Move -- Planning Attacks
bullets: Look for battles with overwhelming force or high strategic value
Common Soviet opening: attack Ukraine and/or West Russia
Goal: trade territories for income, weaken German forward positions

summary.title: Executing an Attack on Ukraine
bullets: Move infantry, artillery, tanks from Caucasus into Ukraine
Send fighters from Moscow (move 4 -- enough to return after combat)
All attacking units enter Ukraine during Combat Move

summary.title: The Ukraine Battle
bullets: Place units on battle strip aligned with combat values
Attack values: Infantry 1 (2 with artillery), Artillery 2, Tank 3, Fighter 3
Defense values: Infantry 2, Tank 3, Fighter 4
Roll dice for each group, count hits

summary.title: Resolving Casualties
bullets: Defender chooses own casualties, places behind casualty strip
Casualties still fire in step 4 before removal in step 5
Attacker loses cheapest units first: infantry > artillery > tanks > fighters

summary.title: Winning the Battle
bullets: Capture territory if all defenders eliminated and you have surviving land units
Place control marker, adjust national production levels
If defenders remain: press attack or retreat all units to a single friendly territory

summary.title: Common Mistake 1: Forgetting AA Gun Fire
bullets: AA guns fire BEFORE first combat round at attacking air units
Each AA gun fires up to 3 shots; each air unit targeted only once; hit on 1
Destroyed aircraft removed immediately -- never get to attack

summary.title: Phase 4: Noncombat Move
bullets: Move non-combatant units to reinforce front lines
Land fighters in safe territories within remaining range
Move AA guns forward (they can ONLY move in this phase)
Stack units in Moscow, Caucasus, Karelia as a defensive wall

summary.title: Common Mistake 2: Transport Loading Rules
bullets: Transport capacity: 1 land unit + 1 additional infantry
Loading/offloading = unit's ENTIRE movement
Combat Move loading MUST lead to amphibious assault -- cannot sit at sea
Cannot offload if sea zone has enemy subs and you have no warship escort

summary.title: Phase 5: Mobilize New Units
bullets: Deploy 8 infantry at Moscow (up to 8 units) and/or Karelia (up to 2)
Moscow placement provides central defensive position
Only use complexes controlled at START of turn

summary.title: Phase 6: Collect Income
bullets: Collect IPCs equal to total value of all controlled territories
Income changes as territories are captured and lost
USSR starting 24 + captured Ukraine (3) = 27 income (before German counterattacks)

summary.title: Common Mistake 3: Amphibious Assault Bombardment Errors
bullets: Bombardment only if NO sea combat occurred in the sea zone
Even attacking enemy subs/transports counts as sea combat and cancels bombardment
Max bombarding ships = number of land units offloading

summary.title: Germany's Turn -- The Hammer Falls
bullets: Germany has 41 IPCs and the strongest military on the board
Watch for combined arms: infantry + artillery (boosted attack), tanks blitzing, air strikes
Your infantry wall should slow the advance

summary.title: United Kingdom's Turn
bullets: UK has 31 IPCs, forces scattered across Britain, Egypt, India
Common strategy: build transports + infantry to threaten Western Europe
Amphibious assault threat forces Germany to divert defenders

summary.title: Japan's Turn
bullets: Japan has 30 IPCs with a massive navy and expanding empire
Pushes into China, threatens India, deals with US Pacific fleet
Strategic choice: pressure USSR from east vs. expand in Pacific

summary.title: United States' Turn
bullets: US has 42 IPCs -- highest income, but forces far from fighting
Key decision: Atlantic first (help vs Germany) or Pacific first (contain Japan)
After US turn, check victory city count; if no winner, new round begins

summary.title: Understanding the Economic Cycle
bullets: Territories > IPCs > Units > Capture territories > More IPCs
Allied combined income (97) vs Axis (71) -- Allies win the long game
Axis must strike fast before Allied production overwhelms them

summary.title: The Infantry Push Mechanic
bullets: Buy infantry every turn, push toward front lines
Infantry absorb hits (cheap at 3 IPCs, defend at 2)
Keep tanks and fighters behind the lines for counterattacks
This is the core tactic for all powers, especially USSR

summary.title: Turn 2: USSR Again
bullets: Expect Germany to recapture some territory -- Eastern Front is a grind
Consider adding 1 artillery (4 IPCs) to infantry purchases for better attacks
Apply the full six-phase sequence with growing confidence

summary.title: Congratulations
bullets: You have completed a full round plus start of round 2
Three key mistakes: AA fire, transport rules, bombardment conditions
You understand the six-phase turn, combat, and economic engine

summary.title: Before You Go to War
bullets: Check General Tips for strategic fundamentals
Reference the Appendix for every unit type, cost, and special ability
Ask your personal GM Guide anytime via the Q&A tab

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before diving into strategy, here are the most common mistakes that derail new Axis & Allies players. First, buying too many expensive units early -- tanks and planes are flashy, but infantry win wars through sheer numbers and cost-efficiency. Second, forgetting that AA guns fire at your aircraft before combat begins, leading to unpleasant surprises. Third, misusing transports: loading during Combat Move without conducting an amphibious assault, or offloading without a warship escort when enemy subs are present. Fourth, sending air units beyond their range and losing them. Fifth, ignoring the economic game -- every territory you lose costs you IPCs and gives them to the enemy.

walkthrough.title: The Economy Wins Wars
walkthrough.text: Axis & Allies is fundamentally an economic game. The side that produces more units over time will eventually overwhelm the other. Every territory you hold contributes IPCs to your war machine. Every territory you lose subtracts from yours and adds to theirs. This means territory trading -- attacking to capture a territory, knowing the enemy will recapture it, then taking it back again -- is a core tactic. Each trade costs both sides units, but if you trade efficiently (losing fewer IPCs worth of units than your opponent), you come out ahead over time. Track the economic balance as closely as the military one.

walkthrough.title: Infantry Are Your Foundation
walkthrough.text: Buy infantry every single turn. No exceptions for the first several rounds. Infantry cost 3 IPCs, defend at 2, and absorb one hit just like a 20-IPC battleship. They are the backbone of both offense and defense. When attacking, pair them with artillery (which boosts their attack from 1 to 2) and use tanks and fighters to deliver the killing blows. When defending, stack them deep -- a wall of 10 infantry defending at 2 each is far more durable than a handful of expensive units. The golden ratio for purchases is roughly 70-80% infantry, with the rest split between artillery, tanks, and air power.

walkthrough.title: Combined Arms Offense
walkthrough.text: On offense, combined arms is everything. Infantry alone attack at a measly 1 -- they need support. One artillery boosts one infantry to attack at 2, effectively doubling that infantry's combat power for just 1 extra IPC. Tanks attack at 3 and have move 2 for blitzing. Fighters attack at 3 with move 4 for reaching distant battles. Bombers attack at 4 with move 6, making them the heaviest hitters. The ideal attacking force combines cheap infantry to absorb casualties with artillery support and mobile units (tanks, planes) for firepower. Never attack with expensive units alone -- always bring infantry as hit absorbers.

walkthrough.title: Protect Your Capital
walkthrough.text: Losing your capital is catastrophic. The enemy seizes all your unspent IPCs, and you cannot collect income or purchase units until it is liberated. For the Allies, Moscow is the most vulnerable capital early in the game. Germany can concentrate overwhelming force against it. Keep Moscow heavily garrisoned at all times -- ideally 15 to 20 or more infantry plus fighters. For the Axis, Berlin becomes vulnerable once the Allies establish a foothold in Western Europe. Tokyo is usually safer due to naval distance. Spend your IPCs before your turn ends if your capital is threatened -- the enemy cannot steal what you already spent.

walkthrough.title: Air Power Is Mobile Firepower
walkthrough.text: Fighters and bombers are expensive but incredibly versatile. A fighter (cost 10, attack 3, defense 4, move 4) can reach battles across a wide area and defends better than any land unit except a same-cost combination of infantry. A bomber (cost 12, attack 4, defense 1, move 6) is a devastating attacker that can also conduct strategic bombing raids against enemy industrial complexes. The key to air power is positioning: keep your planes centrally located so they can respond to threats in multiple directions. After combat, they fly back to a safe territory, ready to deploy elsewhere next turn. Do not leave them exposed on the front line where they could be destroyed as casualties in a defensive battle.

walkthrough.title: Naval Strategy Basics
walkthrough.text: Naval power determines who controls the sea lanes and the ability to project land forces via amphibious assault. Destroyers (cost 8, attack 2, defense 2) are essential for countering enemy submarines -- they cancel Surprise Strike, Submersible, and air immunity. Submarines (cost 6, attack 2, defense 1) are cheap harassers that can sneak past enemy fleets. Carriers (cost 14, attack 1, defense 2) carry two fighters, making a carrier plus two fighters a 34-IPC package with excellent defensive power. Battleships (cost 20, attack 4, defense 4) absorb two hits and can bombard in amphibious assaults. Build naval forces to match your strategic needs -- the UK and US need Atlantic fleets to threaten Europe, while Japan must maintain Pacific dominance.

walkthrough.title: Coordinate as Allies
walkthrough.text: If you are playing the Allies, coordination between the three powers is essential. The Soviet Union holds the Eastern Front with a wall of infantry. The United Kingdom builds transports and threatens Western Europe with amphibious assaults, forcing Germany to split its forces. The United States -- the economic giant -- decides whether to focus Atlantic or Pacific, ideally pressuring both. The Allied advantage is economic: 97 combined IPCs versus the Axis 71. But the Allies start geographically separated, which the Axis can exploit. Communicate your plans. Time your attacks so Germany and Japan face pressure on multiple fronts simultaneously. One uncoordinated Allied power can lose the war for the whole team.

walkthrough.title: Play as the Axis: Strike Fast
walkthrough.text: If you are playing the Axis, you are in a race against time. Your combined income of 71 IPCs cannot keep pace with the Allies' 97 forever. Germany must pressure Moscow relentlessly while defending Western Europe from amphibious assault. Japan should expand aggressively in the Pacific and Asia, capturing high-value territories to close the income gap. The ideal Axis strategy is to capture enough victory cities within the first 5-7 rounds before Allied production overwhelms you. Do not play defensively as the Axis -- you will slowly lose the economic war. Be aggressive, take calculated risks, and keep the Allies reacting to your moves rather than the other way around.

walkthrough.title: The Tempo of the Game
walkthrough.text: Axis & Allies has a natural tempo. Rounds 1-3 are the Axis offensive: Germany pushes east, Japan expands everywhere. Rounds 4-6 are the turning point: Allied production catches up, the US fleet deploys, and the UK threatens Europe. Rounds 7 and beyond favor the Allies as their economic engine overwhelms Axis defenses. Understanding this tempo helps you plan. As the Axis, maximize gains in the early rounds. As the Allies, survive the early onslaught and build for the counterpunch. The best games of Axis & Allies come down to whether the Axis can close the deal before the Allied tide becomes unstoppable.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Buying too many expensive units early -- infantry win wars
Forgetting AA gun fire before combat
Misusing transports (loading without amphibious assault, no escort vs subs)
Sending air units beyond range; ignoring the economic balance

summary.title: The Economy Wins Wars
bullets: Every territory = IPCs; losing one costs you and benefits the enemy
Territory trading is a core tactic -- trade efficiently to grind the opponent down
Track economic balance (income totals) as closely as military positions

summary.title: Infantry Are Your Foundation
bullets: Cost 3 IPCs, defend at 2 -- best cost-per-defense ratio in the game
Absorb hits like expensive units but cost a fraction of the price
Buy 70-80% infantry with rest split among artillery, tanks, air power
10 infantry defending at 2 each >>> a few expensive units

summary.title: Combined Arms Offense
bullets: Artillery boosts paired infantry attack from 1 to 2 (one-for-one)
Tanks: attack 3, move 2 for blitzing; Fighters: attack 3, move 4; Bombers: attack 4, move 6
Always bring cheap infantry as hit absorbers alongside expensive firepower

summary.title: Protect Your Capital
bullets: Losing capital = lose all IPCs, no income, no purchases until liberated
Moscow most vulnerable early; Berlin vulnerable once Allies land in Europe
Keep capitals garrisoned with 15-20+ infantry and fighters
Spend IPCs before turn ends if capital is threatened

summary.title: Air Power Is Mobile Firepower
bullets: Fighters: cost 10, attack 3, defense 4, move 4 -- best defensive value
Bombers: cost 12, attack 4, defense 1, move 6 -- can also strategic bomb
Position centrally for maximum reach; fly back after combat
Do not leave on exposed front lines

summary.title: Naval Strategy Basics
bullets: Destroyers (8 IPCs) cancel sub abilities -- essential anti-sub escort
Carrier + 2 fighters (34 IPCs total) = excellent defensive naval platform
Subs (6 IPCs) cheap harassers that pass through enemy fleets
Battleships (20 IPCs): 2 hits to destroy, can bombard in amphibious assaults

summary.title: Coordinate as Allies
bullets: USSR holds the East with infantry; UK threatens Western Europe amphibiously
US decides Atlantic vs Pacific focus -- ideally pressures both
Allied economic advantage: 97 combined IPCs vs Axis 71
Coordinate timing so Germany and Japan face simultaneous pressure

summary.title: Play as the Axis: Strike Fast
bullets: Axis are in a race against time -- 71 IPCs cannot outproduce 97 long-term
Germany must pressure Moscow; Japan must expand aggressively
Aim to capture enough victory cities in rounds 5-7
Do not play defensively -- you will slowly lose the economic war

summary.title: The Tempo of the Game
bullets: Rounds 1-3: Axis offensive peak, maximum expansion
Rounds 4-6: turning point, Allied production catches up
Round 7+: Allied economic engine overwhelms Axis
Axis must close the deal before the tide turns

walkthrough.title: German Opening Moves
walkthrough.text: Germany's first turn sets the tone for the entire game. With 41 IPCs and the strongest military, Germany should purchase a mix of infantry and tanks (e.g., 5 infantry + 3 tanks = 15 + 18 = 33 IPCs, or 8 infantry + 1 artillery + 1 tank = 24 + 4 + 6 = 34 IPCs). The key attacks on turn 1 are: recapture Ukraine if the USSR took it, push into Karelia, potentially take Egypt to control the Suez Canal, and consider a submarine raid against the British navy. Germany must be careful not to overextend -- every unit lost on offense is one fewer defending Berlin.

walkthrough.title: The Moscow Crush Strategy
walkthrough.text: The most direct Axis strategy is a concentrated push to capture Moscow. Germany dedicates the vast majority of its production to the Eastern Front, buying infantry and tanks every turn and marching them east. The goal is to reach Moscow by round 4-5 with overwhelming force. This requires Japan to apply pressure from the east, tying down Soviet forces in the Far East. The risk is that the UK and US will use the Atlantic to establish a beachhead in Western Europe while Germany's back is turned. Counter this by leaving just enough infantry and fighters in Western Europe to deter amphibious assaults.

walkthrough.title: Economic Warfare and Strategic Bombing
walkthrough.text: Strategic bombing is a powerful tool for crippling enemy production. A bomber (cost 12, attack 4, move 6) flying a strategic bombing raid rolls one die for damage to an industrial complex. The complex's built-in air defense fires first (hit on 1 per bomber), so there is risk. But if 3 bombers survive and roll well, they can deal 10+ damage points, effectively shutting down a complex for a turn. This is most effective against Moscow (IPC value 8, max damage 16) or Berlin (IPC value 10, max damage 20). The optional escort/interceptor rule adds a layer of air combat before the bombing, making fighters valuable as both escorts and interceptors.

walkthrough.title: Naval Control of the Atlantic
walkthrough.text: Control of the Atlantic is critical for the Allies. The UK needs to build transports and escorts to ferry units to Western Europe or North Africa. The US needs to deploy its massive economy across the ocean. Germany can contest this with submarines (cheap at 6 IPCs) raiding Allied convoys and by maintaining enough air power in Western Europe to threaten Allied naval forces. The Allies should build destroyers to counter subs and carriers for fleet defense. A well-timed Allied amphibious assault on Western Europe or Norway can force Germany to pull units from the Eastern Front, relieving pressure on Moscow.

walkthrough.title: Japan's Pacific Expansion
walkthrough.text: Japan starts with the most powerful navy in the game and should use it aggressively. Key turn 1 moves: destroy the US Pacific fleet (Pearl Harbor), capture the Philippines, push into the East Indies for their high IPC values, and begin pressuring India and China. Japan should build a transport pipeline -- buying transports and infantry to continuously funnel ground forces from Japan to the Asian mainland. Every territory Japan captures increases its income toward parity with the Allies. If Japan reaches 50+ IPCs income, the Axis economic disadvantage shrinks dramatically.

walkthrough.title: The Kill Germany First (KGF) Strategy
walkthrough.text: The classic Allied strategy is Kill Germany First. The USSR defends Moscow with maximum infantry purchases. The UK builds a transport fleet in the Atlantic and begins landing units in Western Europe or Norway every turn. The US commits most of its production to the Atlantic -- carriers, transports, infantry, and aircraft. The combined UK-US pressure forces Germany to defend both east and west simultaneously, splitting its forces. Meanwhile, Japan is largely ignored in the Pacific. The risk is that Japan grows unchecked and captures enough victory cities to win. KGF requires speed -- Germany must fall before Japan's expansion becomes decisive.

walkthrough.title: The Kill Japan First (KJF) Strategy
walkthrough.text: A less common but viable strategy is Kill Japan First. The US commits heavily to the Pacific, building carriers, battleships, destroyers, and transports. The goal is to destroy Japan's navy and then island-hop toward Tokyo. The UK focuses on holding India and supporting the Pacific push. The USSR must hold against Germany largely alone, which means buying nothing but infantry and retreating as needed. The advantage of KJF is that Japan is harder to rebuild once its navy is destroyed (naval units are expensive), while Germany can be contained by a defensive USSR. The risk is that Germany captures Moscow before Japan falls.

walkthrough.title: The Deadzone and Trading Territories
walkthrough.text: A deadzone is a territory that sits between two large opposing forces where neither side can hold it safely. Both sides can attack into it but neither can defend it against the counterattack. This creates a territory trade: you capture it on your turn, the enemy recaptures it on theirs. The key is to trade efficiently -- lose fewer IPCs of units than your opponent each exchange. Use infantry as the front-line attackers (cheap and expendable) with tanks and fighters held back for counterstrikes. The Eastern Front naturally creates deadzones in Ukraine, West Russia, and Belorussia. Mastering the deadzone trade is essential for competitive play.

walkthrough.title: Carrier Groups and Fleet Composition
walkthrough.text: The most efficient naval defensive platform is the carrier group: one aircraft carrier (14 IPCs) loaded with two fighters (10 each = 20 IPCs) for a total of 34 IPCs. This gives you defense values of 2 (carrier) + 4 + 4 (fighters) = 10 defense points across three units that absorb three hits. Compare to a battleship (20 IPCs): defense 4 but absorbs only two hits. The carrier group is more expensive total but far more efficient per IPC. For offense, add destroyers (to counter subs), cruisers (for bombardment), and submarines (cheap attack at 2). A balanced fleet has carriers for defense, destroyers for sub hunting, and transports for amphibious assaults.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Victory City Counting
walkthrough.text: As the game enters the mid-to-late rounds, victory cities become the scoreboard. Axis start with 6 (Berlin, Paris, Rome, Shanghai, Manila, Tokyo) and need 9 for Standard Victory. The most contestable Allied cities are Leningrad, Moscow, Calcutta, and Honolulu. Germany targets Leningrad and Moscow. Japan targets Calcutta, Honolulu, and possibly San Francisco. If the Axis can capture any 3 of these, they win. Conversely, the Allies need 10 -- they must hold all 7 of their own and capture 3 Axis cities. The most vulnerable Axis cities are Paris, Rome, and Manila. Always count victory cities at the end of each round and plan your strategy around which cities are contestable.

summary.title: German Opening Moves
bullets: Purchase infantry + tanks (e.g., 5 inf + 3 tanks = 33 IPCs)
Turn 1 attacks: recapture Ukraine, push Karelia, consider Egypt, sub raid vs UK navy
Do not overextend -- every unit lost on offense weakens Berlin's defense

summary.title: The Moscow Crush Strategy
bullets: Concentrate all German production toward Eastern Front
Target Moscow capture by round 4-5 with overwhelming force
Japan applies pressure from the east to tie down Soviet forces
Leave minimal defense in Western Europe to deter amphibious assaults

summary.title: Economic Warfare and Strategic Bombing
bullets: Bombers roll 1 die each for damage to industrial complexes
Complex air defense fires first: 1 die per bomber, hit on 1
Max damage = 2x territory IPC value; each chip reduces production by 1
Most effective vs Moscow (max 16 damage) or Berlin (max 20 damage)

summary.title: Naval Control of the Atlantic
bullets: Allies need transports + escorts to ferry units to Western Europe
Germany contests with cheap submarines (6 IPCs) and air power
Destroyers essential to counter sub Surprise Strike and Submersible
Allied amphibious threat forces Germany to split east/west defenses

summary.title: Japan's Pacific Expansion
bullets: Turn 1: destroy US Pacific fleet, capture Philippines and East Indies
Build transport pipeline to funnel infantry from Japan to Asian mainland
Target 50+ IPCs income to shrink Axis economic disadvantage
Pressure India and China while maintaining naval dominance

summary.title: Kill Germany First (KGF) Strategy
bullets: USSR buys max infantry to hold Moscow; UK builds transport fleet
US commits most production to Atlantic (carriers, transports, infantry)
Combined UK-US pressure forces Germany to split east/west
Risk: Japan grows unchecked -- must defeat Germany before Japan wins

summary.title: Kill Japan First (KJF) Strategy
bullets: US builds Pacific fleet: carriers, battleships, destroyers, transports
Destroy Japan's navy then island-hop toward Tokyo
USSR must hold Germany alone with pure infantry buys
Risk: Germany captures Moscow before Japan falls

summary.title: The Deadzone and Trading Territories
bullets: Deadzones: territories neither side can hold against counterattack
Trade efficiently -- lose fewer IPCs worth of units per exchange
Use infantry as expendable front-line, tanks/fighters as counterstrikers
Eastern Front deadzones: Ukraine, West Russia, Belorussia

summary.title: Carrier Groups and Fleet Composition
bullets: Carrier + 2 fighters (34 IPCs) = 10 defense points, absorbs 3 hits
Battleship (20 IPCs) = 4 defense, absorbs 2 hits -- less efficient
Add destroyers for sub hunting, cruisers for bombardment
Balanced fleet: carriers (defense) + destroyers (anti-sub) + transports (assault)

summary.title: Endgame Victory City Counting
bullets: Axis need 9 cities (start with 6): target Leningrad, Moscow, Calcutta, Honolulu
Allies need 10 cities (start with 7): target Paris, Rome, Manila
Count victory cities every round and plan strategy around contestable cities
Games are won and lost by controlling the right cities, not the most territory

entries.definition: The cheapest combat unit. Cost: 3 IPCs. Attack: 1 (2 when supported by artillery). Defense: 2. Move: 1. Infantry are the backbone of every army -- best cost-per-defense ratio in the game. Can be carried by transports.

Support unit that boosts infantry. Cost: 4 IPCs. Attack: 2. Defense: 2. Move: 1. Each artillery supports one infantry, raising that infantry's attack from 1 to 2. Does not support infantry on defense. Can be carried by transports.

Heavy mobile land unit. Cost: 6 IPCs. Attack: 3. Defense: 3. Move: 2. Tanks can blitz through unoccupied hostile territories, claiming them and continuing to a second space. Must stop if the first territory contains any enemy units. Can be carried by transports.

Defensive anti-air unit. Cost: 5 IPCs. Attack: none. Defense: 0 combat value (special air defense). Move: 1 (Noncombat Move phase only). Each AA gun fires up to 3 shots at attacking air units before the first round of combat, hitting on a roll of 1. Cannot fire during normal combat steps. Can be taken as a casualty. Can be transported.

Versatile air unit. Cost: 10 IPCs. Attack: 3. Defense: 4. Move: 4. Must reserve movement for return to a safe landing spot. Can land on friendly aircraft carriers (up to 2 per carrier). Can fly through hostile spaces. Cannot hit submarines unless a friendly destroyer is in the battle.

Heavy air unit. Cost: 12 IPCs. Attack: 4. Defense: 1. Move: 6. Can participate in normal combat or conduct strategic bombing raids against enemy industrial complexes. Must land in a friendly territory (not on carriers). Cannot hit submarines unless a friendly destroyer is present.

Stealth sea unit. Cost: 6 IPCs. Attack: 2. Defense: 1. Move: 2. Has Surprise Strike (fires before other units unless enemy destroyer present), Submersible (can exit combat by submerging), treats hostile sea zones as friendly (except where enemy destroyers are), cannot hit or be hit by air units (unless enemy destroyer present), does not block enemy movement.

Cargo vessel. Cost: 7 IPCs. Attack: 0. Defense: 0. Move: 2. Carries one land unit plus one additional infantry (max: 2 infantry, or 1 tank/artillery/AA gun + 1 infantry). No combat value -- cannot fire in combat. Chosen as casualty last. Does not block enemy movement. Loading/offloading counts as the land unit's entire move.

Anti-submarine warship. Cost: 8 IPCs. Attack: 2. Defense: 2. Move: 2. Cancels enemy submarine abilities: Surprise Strike, Submersible, and immunity to air hits. Forces enemy submarines to stop upon entering the destroyer's sea zone. Essential escort for any fleet facing submarines.

Medium warship. Cost: 12 IPCs. Attack: 3. Defense: 3. Move: 2. Can conduct offshore bombardment during amphibious assaults, hitting on a roll of 3 or less. One cruiser can bombard per offloaded land unit.

Fighter platform. Cost: 14 IPCs. Attack: 1. Defense: 2. Move: 2. Carries up to 2 fighters (including those of friendly powers). Fighters launch before the carrier moves. Guest fighters on allied carriers are cargo -- they cannot fight and are destroyed if the carrier sinks. A carrier + 2 fighters is the most cost-efficient naval defensive platform.

The heaviest warship. Cost: 20 IPCs. Attack: 4. Defense: 4. Move: 2. Takes 2 hits to destroy -- tilt on side after first hit, repairs at end of combat if it survives. Can conduct offshore bombardment during amphibious assaults, hitting on 4 or less. One battleship can bombard per offloaded land unit.

Production facility. Cost: 15 IPCs. Cannot attack, defend in combat, or move. Entry point for all purchased units. Production limit equals territory IPC value minus damage. Has built-in air defense against strategic bombing: rolls 1 die per attacking bomber, hit on 1. Cannot be destroyed, only damaged. Max damage = 2x territory IPC value. Repairs cost 1 IPC per damage point. Captured with the territory.

The currency of Axis & Allies. Spent to purchase units and repair industrial complexes. Collected as income equal to the total IPC value of territories you control. Cannot be lent or given to allies. Seized by the enemy when they capture your capital.

The total IPC income a power generates per turn, tracked on the national production chart. Increases when you capture territories, decreases when you lose them. Determines IPC income collected in Phase 6.

The pool of unspent IPCs a power has available. Starts equal to the power's national production level. Tracked by the banker. Unspent IPCs carry over between turns. All IPCs are seized if your capital is captured.

One of 13 strategic cities that determine the winner. Allied cities: Washington, London, Leningrad, Moscow, Calcutta, Honolulu, San Francisco. Axis cities: Berlin, Paris, Rome, Shanghai, Manila, Tokyo. Standard Victory: Axis 9 / Allies 10. Total Victory: 13.

The default win condition. The Axis win by controlling 9 or more victory cities (3 more than their starting 6). The Allies win by controlling 10 or more (3 more than their starting 7). Checked at the end of each round after the US turn.
The optional win condition. One side must control all 13 victory cities. Must be agreed upon before the game begins. Results in a longer, more decisive game.

The six phases each power completes in order: 1. Purchase Units, 2. Combat Move, 3. Conduct Combat, 4. Noncombat Move, 5. Mobilize New Units, 6. Collect Income. Collect Income is mandatory; all others are voluntary.
The fixed sequence in which powers take turns each round: 1. Soviet Union, 2. Germany, 3. United Kingdom, 4. Japan, 5. United States, then check for victory. This order never changes.

Phase 2 of the turn. Move units into hostile spaces to attack. All movement is simultaneous. Land units stop upon entering hostile territory. Sea units stop upon encountering enemy surface warships. Air units must reserve movement for the return trip.

Phase 4 of the turn. Move units that did not participate in combat. Land surviving aircraft. Land units can only enter friendly territories. Air units use remaining movement to reach a safe landing spot. AA guns can only move in this phase. Submarines can move through hostile sea zones.

Phase 5 of the turn. Deploy all purchased units at eligible industrial complexes. Production limit = territory IPC value minus damage chips. Sea units placed in adjacent sea zones. Cannot use complexes captured or purchased this turn.

A special tank movement. A tank can move through an unoccupied hostile territory (claiming it), then continue to a second space which can be friendly or hostile. The entire move occurs during Combat Move. The tank cannot blitz if the first territory contains any enemy units (including AA guns or industrial complexes).

A submarine combat ability. Before all other units fire, submarines can roll their attack or defense. Attacking subs hit on 2 or less, defending subs hit on 1. Casualties from Surprise Strikes are removed immediately (do not return fire). Cancelled if an enemy destroyer is present in the battle.

A submarine's ability to exit combat by submerging. Can be chosen instead of making a Surprise Strike. The sub is immediately removed from the battle strip and returned to the board. Cannot submerge if an enemy destroyer is present. Decision is made before any dice are rolled.

During an amphibious assault, battleships and cruisers in the sea zone can bombard the coastal territory being attacked. Battleships hit on 4 or less, cruisers on 3 or less. The number of bombarding ships is limited to the number of land units being offloaded. Bombardment is cancelled if any sea combat occurred in the sea zone.

A bomber attack on an enemy industrial complex. The complex's air defense fires first (1 die per bomber, hit on 1 = bomber destroyed). Surviving bombers each roll 1 die for damage. Damage chips reduce the complex's production capacity. Max total damage = 2x territory IPC value. Repairs cost 1 IPC per chip. Bombers cannot participate in other combat the same turn.

An attack on a coastal territory using land units offloaded from transports. Announced during Combat Move. Sequence: sea combat (if enemy warships present), bombardment (if no sea combat occurred), land combat. Seaborne units cannot retreat. Must be announced before combat begins.

A reference card used during combat. Units are placed on their respective Attacker or Defender sides, aligned with their combat values. Columns show each unit type's attack and defense numbers. Roll equal to or less than the column number to score a hit.

A card placed adjacent to the battle strip. Defending units that have been hit are moved behind it. These casualties still fire in the defending units fire step before being removed from the game in step 5.

A battleship ability. Battleships require two hits in the same combat to be destroyed. After the first hit, tilt the battleship on its side. If it survives the combat, it repairs immediately and is returned upright.

AA guns fire before the first round of combat at attacking air units. Total dice = lesser of (3 x number of AA guns) or (number of attacking air units). Each hit on a roll of 1 destroys one air unit immediately. Does not apply to territories being flown over, only the attacked territory.

Industrial complexes have built-in air defense during strategic bombing raids. Roll 1 die per attacking bomber; hit on 1 = bomber destroyed immediately. Separate from AA gun fire -- AA guns do not defend against strategic bombing. If the optional escort/interceptor rule is used, an air battle occurs before the complex fires.
A territory controlled by you or a power on your side. Your units can move freely through friendly territories. You collect income from territories you control.
A territory controlled by an enemy power. Land units must stop upon entering hostile territory. Entering hostile territory triggers combat. Air units can pass through hostile territories freely.

A territory not controlled by any power (e.g., Turkey, Mongolia, Sahara). Impassable for all units -- you cannot attack, move through, or fly over neutral territories. They have no income value and control never changes.

A sea zone containing no enemy surface warships (battleships, carriers, cruisers, or destroyers). Enemy submarines and transports alone do NOT make a sea zone hostile. Your units can move through friendly sea zones.

A sea zone containing enemy surface warships (battleships, carriers, cruisers, or destroyers). Your sea units must stop upon entering a hostile sea zone. Submarines can pass through hostile sea zones unless an enemy destroyer is present.

Connects sea zone 19 (Pacific) and sea zone 18 (Atlantic). Controlled by the side that holds Central America. Sea units can only pass through if your side controlled it at the start of your turn. Air units are unaffected. Land movement within Central America is not affected.

Connects sea zone 17 (Mediterranean) and sea zone 34 (Indian Ocean). Your side must control both Egypt and Trans-Jordan to use it. If one side holds Egypt and the other holds Trans-Jordan, the canal is closed. Air units are unaffected.

A territory surrounded entirely by one or more sea zones. An island group within a sea zone is treated as one territory. Land units on islands cannot be split across different islands in the same group. Unnamed islands are not game spaces.

The home territory of a power: Washington (US), Moscow (USSR), London (UK), Berlin (Germany), Tokyo (Japan). Capturing an enemy capital seizes all their unspent IPCs. The conquered power cannot collect income or buy units until the capital is liberated.

When you capture a territory originally controlled by an ally, the original controller regains it (liberation). You do not keep it. Exception: if the original controller's capital is enemy-held, you capture it instead. When a capital is liberated, all territories originally belonging to that power revert to their original controller.

Units from the same side sharing a territory or sea zone. Multinational forces can defend together but cannot attack together -- each power fights only on its own turn. Allied units in a sea zone where an attack occurs do not participate. Attacking fighters can launch from allied carriers, but allied carriers do not move until their owner's turn.

A transport carries one land unit plus one additional infantry. Maximum load: 2 infantry, or 1 tank/artillery/AA gun + 1 infantry. Cannot carry industrial complexes. Loading/offloading uses the land unit's entire movement. All cargo loaded during Combat Move must offload as an amphibious assault.

An aircraft carrier holds up to 2 fighters, including those belonging to friendly powers. Fighters belonging to the carrier's owner launch before the carrier moves and act independently. Guest fighters from allies are treated as cargo -- they cannot fight and are destroyed if the carrier sinks.

In sea combat, if the defender has only transports remaining (no combat units that can fire at the attacker), those transports and their cargo are automatically destroyed without further dice rolls. This also applies when remaining combat units cannot hit each other (e.g., defender has only subs and transports, attacker has only air units).

Used to stack units in crowded territories. Gray chip = 1 unit, green chip = 3 units, red chip = 5 units. Place a unit sculpt on top of the chip stack. Also used as damage markers under industrial complexes during strategic bombing.
Round tokens showing a power's emblem. Used to mark conquered territories and to indicate national production level on the production chart. Take all markers for your power at setup.
Reference card showing a power's name, color, emblem, and the exact starting positions of all its units. Used during setup to place units correctly on the board.
An area on the game board where newly purchased units are placed before being deployed. Units remain here until Phase 5 (Mobilize New Units) of the current turn.
